title: Wealth and Insurance Choices: Evidence from U.S. Households
abstract: Using administrative data for 63,000 individuals across 2,500,000 person-month observations, we find that wealthier individuals have better life insurance coverage, controlling for the value of the asset insured, namely, the consumption needs of dependents. This positive wealth-insurance correlation, which is surprising given the prevailing view that wealth substitutes for insurance, persists after allowing for wealth-related differences in risk or bequest preferences, pricing, background risk, education, employment, or liquidity constraints. Our findings call for further investigation of this wealth-coverage correlation but support theories emphasizing the consumption-smoothing role of insurance across not only states of the world, but also time.

article.introduction:
Canonical models of insurance demandsuch as Mossin (1968) and Lewis (1989), as well as the more recent models in Gollier (2003) and Koijen, Van Nieuwerburgh, and Yogo (2016), focus on the role of insurance in smoothing consumption across states of the world. These theories posit that one of the key factors driving insurance purchases is wealth. Specifically, the prediction is that, all else equal, wealthier individuals, who have greater risk-bearing capacity, will purchase less insurance, as insurance products come with a “load,” that is, monitoring and other expenses that make them costlier than self-insurance through saving.
In this paper, we test this prediction using novel administrative data on life insurance purchases. Contrary to what the standard models predict, we find that wealthier people buy more insurance. To shed light on this puzzling result, we begin by investigating a number of frictions that are not explicitly modeled, to approach the “all else equal” condition assumed by theory. Specifically, we investigate whether the positive wealth-coverage correlation that we document is driven by the existence of wealth-related differences in risk preferences, pricing, background risk, bequest motives, financial literacy, subjective beliefs or adverse selection, or access to employer-provided life insurance. We find that some of these channels do explain a small portion of the positive wealth-coverage correlation, but we continue to observe that the wealthy are better insured even after accounting for these factors.
Importantly, we also find evidence consistent with the recent theoretical view of insurance proposed in Ericson and Sydnor (2018), Casaburi and Willis (2018), and Rampini and Viswanathan (2019), who underscore the fact that insurance transfers resources across time, not just across states of the world. When insurance is viewed as state-contingent savings over time, where premiums are paid up front, one can obtain the prediction that wealthier people will buy more insurance because less wealthy individuals do not have access to sufficient liquidity at the time when they would want buy insurance claims. In our data, we find that people who are liquidity-constrained do indeed buy less insurance. However, again, even accounting for this channel, we continue to observe that wealthier people buy more coverage.
Overall, in this paper, we provide novel evidence based on administrative data regarding the effects of wealth on insurance decisions, and we investigate several mechanisms not previously studied empirically that could help explain the positive wealth-coverage relationship. While our results support theoretical models that view insurance as a means to smooth consumption across not only states, but also across time, the frictions that we investigate cannot fully explain this positive correlation, highlighting the importance of further empirical work on this important part of households' financial portfolio.
Our data consist of the administrative records of 63,000 people over the period 2015 to 2019, for a total of 2,500,000 person-month observations. The individuals in our sample are broadly representative of the middle 50% of the income distribution in the United States, for whom standard models of insurance demand are most applicable.1We focus on individuals' demand for term life insurance. Term life insurance is an ideal setting to test theoretical models of insurance demand, as it is a pure insurance contract. This type of policy pays out a prespecified amount (i.e., the coverage limit) in the event that the insured dies during the duration (i.e., term) of the contract, which is specified ex ante.2
In contrast to canonical theory, we observe a strong positive relationship between a person's wealth and the extent of coverage secured through life insurance, controlling for the value of the insured asset, namely, the consumption needs of dependents or the insured's labor income stream. For example, we estimate that a $1 increase in financial wealth leads to a $0.64 increase in a person's term life insurance coverage limit. We further find that wealthier individuals are less likely to let their life insurance policies lapse: A $100,000 increase in financial wealth is associated with a 3.1 percentage point decrease in the probability of lapsation. These cross-sectional estimates are similar to the effects we estimate in within-person regressions using the person-month structure of our data panel.
To shed light on why the data do not match standard theoretical predictions on the effect of wealth on insurance coverage, we examine several frictions or potentially confounding variables that are present in canonical models under the “all else equal” umbrella, or which may contradict some basic assumptions in those models. Specifically, we examine whether the risk preferences of the individuals in our sample may be different from the standard decreasing absolute risk aversion (DARA) preferences assumed in these theoretical settings. We also examine whether there may be wealth-related differences in the pricing of insurance, in the level of background risk faced by individuals, in the strength of individuals' bequest motive, in individuals' subjective beliefs about mortality risk or the extent of adverse selection, in individuals' financial literacy, or in the availability of employer-provided life insurance.
In line with assumptions underlying theories of insurance choice, we find that wealthier households have higher risk tolerance, based on their portfolio allocation to risky versus riskless financial assets. This result implies that the positive wealth-coverage correlation that we document is not driven by the existence of risk preferences that differ from what standard theory assumes. When we examine insurance premiums paid per dollar of coverage to assess whether wealthier households get better prices for insurance products, we find that, if anything, wealthier households pay higher premiums per dollar of insurance coverage, potentially due to having more states of the world to which the coverage applies (i.e., a longer term). We find no wealth-related differences in the strength of bequest motives, which would imply wealth-related differences in demand for life insurance. We find that wealthier households have more volatile consumption, income, and financial wealth, and the higher insurance coverage of wealthier individuals is due in part to facing a different distribution of economic shocks, or background risk, relative to their less wealthy counterparts. We also find that wealth-related differences in financial literacy and employer-provided insurance benefits can explain some of the positive wealth-insurance coverage pattern. Nonetheless, after controlling for all of these factors, we continue to observe that wealthier people are better insured.
We next consider the recent theoretical insight that insurance transfers resources across time, not just across states. These recent theories posit that liquidity constraints can prevent low-wealth individuals from buying the extent of insurance coverage that they would like, as they cannot pay the required premiums up front. Consistent with these theories, we find that the more liquidity-constrained people in our sample, as measured by the extent of their credit card use as a fraction of their credit card limits, have less insurance coverage and are more likely to let their policies lapse.
Overall, we find empirically that even after accounting for various departures from the settings considered in theoretical models of insurance choice, wealthier people have more insurance, controlling for the value of the asset insured.3This result suggests the existence of other factors (perhaps driven by the supply side, which we cannot study in our data) that drive the positive correlation between wealth and insurance coverage. At the same time, the evidence presented here is consistent with many features in existing models of insurance, such as decreasing risk aversion or bequest motives, while suggesting that other features be included in future models of insurance choice, such as wealth-related differences in background risk, or in market frictions such as access to liquidity, or knowledge about financial products.
Prior empirical papers on the relationship between households' wealth or income and insurance demand in the United States are often limited to aggregate data or large-scale surveys, do not simultaneously control for income and wealth, or use noisy proxies for insurance coverage instead of actual dollar coverage. Showers and Shotick (1994) analyze households' expenditures on insurance premiums (aggregating across products) using data from the 1987 Consumer Expenditure Survey and find that households with larger incomes spend more on insurance premiums. A similar relationship between income and premiums is documented in the context of life insurance by Fortune (1973) using aggregate U.S. data from 1964 to 1971. Using data from the 1992 wave of the Health and Retirement Survey (HRS), Eisenhauer and Halek (1999) provide evidence that wealthier households purchase more life insurance coverage than do poorer households. However, the authors restrict their sample to households aged between 51 and 61 years old purchasing life insurance and therefore consider demand on the intensive margin only. Fang and Kung (2021) also use data from the HRS to analyze life insurance purchase and lapsation decisions. They find that individuals with higher incomes are more likely to purchase life insurance and less likely to let their policies lapse. However, like Showers and Shotick (1994), they do not control for household wealth, a key theoretical determinant of insurance purchases.
An important contribution to this literature is a contemporaneous paper by Armantier, Foncel, and Treich (2023), who examine the effect of wealth on insurance choices using survey data from approximately 1,800 participants in the Survey of Consumer Expectations and focus on auto insurance coverage. The authors supplement the survey data from the United States with administrative data on auto insurance coverage from clients of a French bank. The authors use an index of insurance coverage based on ordinal responses to survey questions as their measure of coverage. They find that wealthier people have better car insurance.
We complement this paper and prior empirical literature in two main respects. First, in examining the correlation between financial wealth and insurance coverage, we use administrative data covering a large sample of individuals (63,000), and can simultaneously control for income and wealth. Moreover, we examine a different type of insurance contract—term life insurance—that is particularly well suited for testing canonical insurance demand models. Our administrative data allow us to more precisely measure the insurance-wealth relationship both across and within individuals. In particular, we are able to examine dollar coverage limits and insurance lapsation decisions, two dimensions of insurance choice not examined by Armantier, Foncel, and Treich (2023) or other papers relying on administrative data.
Second, in attempting to explain the positive wealth-coverage correlation, we examine several mechanisms, not studied in previous empirical work using individual-level insurance data. Specifically, we examine wealth-related differences in risk preferences, pricing, the strength of the bequest motive, background risk induced by consumption or income volatility, education, employment, and liquidity constraints. Our analysis adds more evidence that wealthier people are better insured. More importantly, our analysis offers additional insights into the extent of the wealth-insurance relationship and supports the importance of frictions that households may face, such as liquidity constraints, in the decision to change insurance coverage or let their insurance coverage lapse entirely.
Our findings are most consistent with new theories of insurance demand that emphasize the importance of consumption smoothing across not only states, but also across time (Ericson and Sydnor (2018), Casaburi and Willis (2018), and Rampini and Viswanathan (2019)). That being said, our results suggest that factors beyond those previously considered by theory, whether the canonical models or the newer models that view insurance as a means to shift resources across states and time, drive the positive wealth-coverage relationship that we document, as it persists even after accounting for the channels considered in these various theoretical settings. Thus, while we provide novel empirical evidence that wealth-dependent factors such as background risk and liquidity constraints can help explain why wealthier individuals have more coverage, we also show that there is still a puzzling positive wealth-insurance correlation, even after we account for such factors.
The academic literature provides other examples of consumer behavior in insurance markets that is difficult to explain. Sydnor (2010) examines the choice of deductible for homeowners' insurance and shows that households overinsure small risks, as they purchase low deductibles despite the costs being significantly above expected payouts. Koijen, Van Nieuwerburgh, and Yogo (2016) use data from the HRS and document that households fail to rebalance their portfolios away from life insurance and toward annuities as they age. Their finding is consistent with the view that inertia may play a role in households' insurance decisions. Cutler and Zeckhauser (2004) point out that some households may make mistakes, as working-age individuals appear to underpurchase life insurance while older individuals appear to be overinsured by having life insurance in the absence of any dependents. Our paper highlights another pattern that is difficult to explain, namely, that the wealthy buy more insurance than the less wealthy, even after accounting for many frictions not previously examined empirically.
The paper proceeds as follows. In SectionI, we present a brief overview of the canonical theoretical framework used to model life insurance decisions, which yields a clear prediction regarding the effect of wealth on coverage: Controlling for the value of the asset insured (i.e., the consumption of dependents), wealthier people should buy less insurance. We describe our sample in SectionII. In SectionIII, we document that, controlling for the variables present in this theoretical framework, wealthier people have more insurance. This result is surprising in light of the model's predictions. In SectionIV, we examine several mechanisms that capture potential departures from the canonical setting and find that they explain a small part of the positive correlation between wealth and insurance coverage. In SectionV, we conclude and suggest avenues for future work to further investigate supply and demand factors that may influence the insurance dimension of households' portfolios.
article.I. Theoretical Framework and Missing Fac:
Standard models of insurance choice feature the same basic setup: Risk-averse agents face some probability of a shock negatively impacting the value of the asset to be insured (e.g., an income or consumption stream, or a physical asset). These models predict that agents' demand for insurance decreases as they become wealthier as long as the insurance premium carries a positive load and the size of the loss is fixed. This predicted negative wealth-insurance demand relationship holds in static, two-period models as in Mossin (1968) and in a dynamic, multiperiod model with multiple income earners as in Lewis (1989) or Gollier (2003). It also holds in settings in which households' demand for insurance is modeled as a portfolio problem, whereby insurance contracts are purchased alongside other risky assets (e.g., Mayers and Smith Jr (1983), Koijen, Van Nieuwerburgh, and Yogo (2016)). The fundamental economic mechanism for the predicted negative relationship is the same: All else equal, wealthier individuals have greater risk-bearing capacity and hence optimally purchase less insurance, whether the insurance products are considered in isolation or in the context of other financial assets.
Below we provide a brief description of the setup in one of these models—Lewis (1989)—to illustrate how a negative wealth-insurance coverage relation can emerge theoretically in the context of life insurance. The model solves for optimal insurance coverage featuring a binary loss, that is, the breadwinner dies. Here we standardize the notation from Lewis (1989, p. 455), under constant relative risk aversion (CRRA) utility with risk aversionγ$\gamma$. LetC$C$denote the amount of insurance coverage purchased,A$A$the value of the asset to be insured (i.e., the consumption stream of dependents, as in pp. 453–454 of Lewis (1989)), andW$W$the financial wealth of the individual (net of the insured asset). The loss occurs with probabilityπ$\pi$, and for simplicity is assumed to be equal to the value of the asset,A$A$. Finally,λ$\lambda$denotes the insurance “loading factor,” that is, the extent to which the premiums charged are above the insurer's expected payout (λ=1$\lambda = 1$implies actuarially fair insurance).
The model features the fundamental prediction that if insurance is actuarially unfair (λ>1$\lambda >1$), then agents will select a coverage limit that is less than the value of the asset,A$A$(i.e., partial insurance).4The amount of insurance purchased,C$C$, is increasing in the value of the asset being insured,A$A$, and is decreasing in wealth,W$W$. The economic rationale for this prediction is the same: risk-bearing capacity. For a fixed loss distribution, wealthier individuals optimally bear a higher risk of loss (under typically assumed risk preferences).
As we document empirically in SectionIII, some of the relationships predicted by theory do indeed hold in the data. For example, we find clear evidence that, all else equal, asA$A$increases,C$C$increases. Specifically, we find that people have higher life insurance coverage for more valuable assets, that is, if the consumption needs of dependents are higher, or, equivalently, if the stream of income that could be lost is higher. However, we find empirically that the correlation betweenC$C$andW$W$is positive (i.e., wealthier people buy more coverage), instead of negative, as the theory predicts. So what could explain this puzzling result?
To address this question, in SectionIV, we empirically examine the role of frictions or potential confounds not directly accounted for in these theoretical models: risk preferences that may not exhibit DARA, pricing or bequest motives that may differ by wealth level, as well as wealth-related heterogeneity in background risk, subjective beliefs about the likelihood of adverse events, financial literacy, employer-provided insurance, and liquidity constraints.
article.II. Data:
We examine households' demand for insurance using administrative data provided by a financial services firm. The financial services firm offers both insurance and banking services to its customers. The data begin in September 2015, end in March 2019, and cover a sample of approximately 63,000 individuals, yielding 2,500,000 person-month observations. For most of the analysis, we work with data aggregated at the person level, but we use monthly data when assessing within-person variables such as the volatility of an individual's consumption or income. We also estimate regressions including person fixed effects using data at the person-month level.
The individuals in our sample were randomly selected from the universe of clients of this financial institution that maintained an active checking or savings account at this firm throughout the sample period. For all individuals, we have information about the monthly flows in and out of the checking or savings accounts, as well as the account balances. A third-party firm employed by the financial institution categorized transactions from the checking and savings accounts into various monthly payment types, such as credit card payments, mortgage payments, and automobile or other loan payments. The data set also includes the identity of each individual's employer if that person is working. If an individual receives income from Social Security, then that amount is labeled as such and is tabulated separately from labor income.
The data provider separates monthly inflows into individuals' bank account(s) into six categories: earned income, income from social security, income from a non–social security pension or annuity, investment income, transfers from another bank, and tax refunds. We classify the sum of all of these inflows as total income in a given month and the sum of the first three as labor income.
We also observe the balances of investment accounts for the subset of individuals in our sample that use the wealth management arm of this financial services company. Our data do not contain the debt levels so we are unable to calculate net worth. We do, however, observe whether the individual has a mortgage and some other types of debt (e.g., automobile or other installment loans) based on their monthly payments. The types of accounts for which we have balance information include checking accounts, savings accounts, certificates of deposit, tax-qualified and non-tax-qualified brokerage accounts, individual retirement accounts, mutual fund investments, and savings and annuity investments. We also have a measure of financial assets held by the individual that is calculated by the third-party data provider employed by the financial institution in an effort to obtain supplementary information about the institution's clients; we use this measure of financial assets because it is available for all individuals regardless of whether they use the wealth management arm of the financial institution.
We obtain two types of information on individuals' insurance behaviors: monthly insurance-related expenditures, based on observing transactions in these individuals' checking accounts, and insurance coverage limits, based on information from the insurance arm of the financial institution. The expenditure data contain information on life insurance premium payments regardless of the identity of the insurer (i.e., we observe payments for policies offered by insurers other than the institution providing the data). However, we observe coverage limits only for policies issued by the financial institution that provided the data for our study; that is, if an individual purchases an insurance policy from an insurer other than our data provider, we observe the premiums paid but not the coverage limit for that policy. We do not observe the term length of life insurance policies.
Our data contain monthly snapshots of demographic information for each person in the sample, such as the zip code of residence, home ownership status, sex, age, marital status, and whether there are children in the household. Individuals in the sample reside in all 50 states and the District of Columbia, for a total of 2,323 counties and 14,547 zip codes. Figure1shows the geographical dispersion of the individuals in our sample.5Summary statistics for the individuals in the sample are presented in TableI. To eliminate the influence of extreme outliers, we exclude from our regression analyses any individuals who fall below the first percentile or above the 99thpercentile in terms of dollar-denominated covariates (e.g., financial wealth or credit card spending).
We also obtain information regarding the housing wealth of the individuals in our sample. To do so, we use data on the near-universe of transactions and housing market values from Corelogic to estimate individuals' housing wealth, conditional on being a homeowner. One concern with using average house price indices to measure individuals' housing wealth is the potential for measurement error that is positively correlated with our main variable of interest, financial wealth, which would bias estimates. To address this concern, we conservatively assume that individuals with higher income in a zip code positively sort into more expensive homes and estimate their housing wealth as follows.6We first assign each individual in our sample into quintiles of income for their zip code using yearly data from the Census Bureau's American Community Survey five-year tables.7We assign the 20thpercentile of home transaction prices for a particular zip code-month pair from Corelogic to homeowners in the first salary quintile in that zip code, the 30thpercentile of transaction prices to homeowners in the second salary quintile, the 50thpercentile to homeowners in the third salary quintile, the 70thpercentile to homeowners in the fourth quintile, and the 80thpercentile of home transaction prices from Corelogic to homeowners in the highest salary quintile. To address small-sample issues in zip codes with few housing market transactions, we calculate housing wealth percentiles based on transactions in each zip code over the trailing 24 months.8Our Corelogic data begin in 2014, and thus the estimated housing wealth values for the initial months of our sample in late 2015 are based on fewer than 24 months of Corelogic data. To measure the volatility of homeowners' housing wealth, we use the standard deviation of the Zillow home value index (ZHVI) for each month-zip code pair.
We next obtain estimates of individuals' probability of death based on their sex and age from the National Vital Statistic System at the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). Because insurers are licensed and regulated at the state level, we use state fixed effects in our analyses to capture regulatory and other time-invariant differences across states.
To assess the representativeness of our sample, we compare it to the 2016 Survey of Consumer Finances (SCF).9Compared to SCF respondents, individuals in our sample are more likely to be married, have children, and own a home. This is to be expected, as by construction our sample consists of individuals who actively use the banking services of a major financial institution. As TableA.Iin theAppendixshows, the distribution of total income for the individuals in our sample looks similar to that of the SCF—54% of individuals in our sample lie within the 25thand 75thpercentiles of total income in the SCF. However, in terms of earned income and financial wealth, the individuals in our sample tend to resemble the middle of the SCF distribution—70% of individuals have earned income within the middle 50% of the SCF earned income distribution, and 95% of individuals have financial wealth between the 25thand 75thpercentiles of the SCF financial wealth distribution.
article.III. Results: Relationship between Wealt:
In TableII, we investigate the factors that influence individuals' decision to have life insurance. We focus on term life insurance policies, as the vast majority of individuals (97%) in our sample have life insurance have term (as opposed to whole) life policies. Moreover, while whole life insurance comes with investment-related, tax-optimization features that make it difficult to study the insurance-related aspect of the product, term life insurance does not include these confounding features (Brown (2001)).10To operationalize a focus on term life policies, we exclude individuals with whole life insurance from all life insurance–related analyses.
The dependent variable in TableIIis an indicator for whether at any time during our sample period the individual had a life insurance policy. We study lapsation of life insurance policies in TableIII.11We define lapsed policies as those for which an individual paid a premium but failed to make another payment within the subsequent 12 months of our sample.12As described in SectionI, we use the standard framework in Lewis (1989) as the theoretical benchmark guiding our empirical study of life insurance choice. According to that framework, the demand for life insurance depends on the level of consumption needed by the individuals benefiting from the policy, for example, spouses and children. In Lewis (1989), this consumption is the expected value of spending by these beneficiaries (in the case of children, the expected value is taken until the time when the children are assumed to have sufficient income of their own). Life insurance is therefore used to guarantee that if the income of the individual is lost due to death, the level of spending by beneficiaries would occur as if the individual stayed alive and earned their income.
In our analysis, we assume that beneficiaries become financially independent when the insured person reaches age 65, but other age thresholds lead to similar results. We calculate the net present value (NPV) of the spending of beneficiaries as the discounted value of a stream of annual credit card spending equal to that observed in the individual's financial records during our sample, over the number of years left until the person reaches 65. We use an annual discount rate of 1%, but the choice of discount rate does not qualitatively change the results. Since consumption by beneficiaries also includes payments to creditors if the household has a mortgage or other consumer loans, we include among the explanatory variables in TablesIIandIIIindicators for whether the household has such liabilities.
Other theories of life insurance (e.g., Fortune (1973)) consider the future labor income stream of the policyholder, rather than dependents' consumption future to be the insured asset. Our conclusions are unchanged if we control for the NPV of future labor income instead of consumption. In TablesA.IIandA.IIIin theAppendix, we reestimate models of life insurance ownership and coverage using the NPV of future labor income as the insured asset. To calculate the NPV of the labor income stream, we assume that the individual works until age 65 and earns the average annualized income stream that we observe for them throughout the sample period, discounted at a 1% annual rate. As the analyses indicate, whether the insured asset is the NPV of future labor income or the NPV of the future expected consumption of dependents does not change the effect of wealth on the take-up of term life insurance or on the extent of coverage through this product. For brevity, below we refer to the estimates using consumption as the insured asset, following Lewis (1989).
As shown in TableII, the canonical predictions regarding the effect of the future expected spending of dependents on whether the person has life insurance hold in the data. A higher expected spending need of spouses or children leads to a significantly higher probability that an individual has life insurance, regardless of whether that expected spending captures the average credit card expenditures that we observe for this person over the sample period, or whether that expected spending captures mortgage payments or payments on other loans that the individual has during the sample, which will likely need to continue being paid if the individual dies. The estimated effects are both statistically significant and economically meaningful. Increasing expected credit card expenditures (i.e., increasing the value of the consumption stream to be insured) by $100,000 is associated with a 0.35 to 0.72 percentage point increase in the probability of having life insurance, depending on the specification. Having a mortgage and dependents at the same time is associated with a 13 to 14 percentage point increase in the probability of having life insurance.13Having dependents and a nonmortgage loan (e.g., an automobile or installment loan) increases the likelihood of owning life insurance by 2 to 4 percentage points. However, as the results in TableIIIdemonstrate, the type of debt payment matters with respect to life insurance lapsation. Mortgage payments are associated with a 2 percentage point decrease in the likelihood of lapsation, whereas nonmortgage loan payments are associated with an increase in the likelihood of lapsation (just over 1 percentage point). These effects are economically sizable, given that the unconditional probability of a person in our sample having term life insurance is 36% (see TableI).
All else equal, the model in Lewis (1989) also predicts that having life insurance is more likely for a higher probability of death and a higher relative risk aversion coefficient.14The first of these predictions can be verified in our data, as for each individual in the sample we are able to merge in the probability of death in the subsequent year based on the person's age and gender. These probabilities are estimated by the CDC and represent averages across populations in the United States that fall into each gender and age subcategories. As the theory predicts, we find a strong and positive correlation between the indicator for whether the person has life insurance and their probability of death in the next year (or equivalently, a negative correlation between having life insurance and the person's expected remaining life span).
The result that does not conform to standard theory is the positive and significant effect of the person's financial wealth on the probability that they have life insurance. Increasing a household's financial wealth by $100,000 is associated with 11 to 13 percentage point increase in the probability of owning life insurance and a 3 percentage point decrease in the probability of a lapse in life insurance coverage. Housing wealth is similarly positively correlated with having life insurance and negatively correlated with lapsation (controlling for having a mortgage), with a $100,000 increase in the value of the home corresponding to a 2 percentage point increase in the probability of having term life insurance and a 0.47 percentage point decrease in lapsation probability.
In TableIV, we examine the factors driving the life insurance coverage limit for individuals in our sample. The dependent variable is equal to the coverage limit for term life insurance policies held by each individual. For those individuals without such policies during the sample period, the dependent variable is equal to zero.
We next investigate the factors that have been proposed theoretically to drive the extent of life insurance in models such as Lewis (1989). We find that for most of these factors, the data line up with the theory. A $1 increase in the insured consumption stream is associated with a $0.04 increase in the life insurance coverage limit. Simultaneously having dependents and a mortgage is associated with increased life insurance holdings of $26,000, while the coefficient on the dependents/nonmortgage loan interaction is no longer significant, indicating that nonmortgage loans drive the extensive margin of life insurance demand but not the intensive margin. The sign on the coefficient estimate for an individual's probability of death switches from positive to negative, which reflects the fact that life insurance ownership is more likely among older individuals but the magnitude of coverage decreases as individuals get older.
However, we find that the effect of wealth on life insurance coverage limits runs counter to the theoretical predictions. Whether financial or housing-related, wealth is a significant, positive predictor of the extent of life insurance coverage. In the full sample (those that have no term life insurance and thus a coverage limit of zero dollars, and those that do have term life coverage for whom we know the coverage limit), we estimate that $1 increase in financial wealth corresponds to a $0.64 increase in their life insurance coverage limit. For housing wealth, a $1 increase leads to a $0.10 increase in their coverage limit. When we restrict the sample to those individuals who have a life insurance policy (see the last column of TableIV), the effects of a $1 increase in wealth on the coverage limit are $1.41 in the case of financial wealth and $0.27 in the case of housing wealth.
Figure2shows that the positive correlation between financial wealth and the extent of term life insurance coverage is present for all levels of financial wealth. Figure2is a binned scatter plot. Binned scatter plots are a convenient way to visualize regression results; they plot the residuals from two multivariate regressions to depict the two-dimensional relationship between the dependent variable and the independent variable of interest in a multivariate regression. In this figure, the residuals from a regression of term life insurance coverage on all explanatory variables except financial wealth are plotted against the residuals from a regression of financial wealth on all other explanatory variables. The data are then grouped into 100 bins and the mean of the residualized values are plotted against each other.15The slope of a hypothetical least squares line plotted in Figure2is identical to the coefficient estimate on financial wealth in the multivariate regression reported in the fourth column of TableIV.
In the analysis presented in Figure2, just as in TableIV, we control for the expected spending of the insured's dependents, the insured individual's probability of death in the subsequent year, their housing wealth, and state fixed effects. As we can see, the correlation between life insurance coverage and financial wealth becomes smaller for higher levels of wealth, but remains positive.
The pattern in Figure2sheds light on an alternative hypothesis for why those with lower wealth buy less life insurance, namely, low-wealth or low-income individuals have access to certain types of social insurance (e.g., government-provided health insurance, food stamps) that may help one's dependents fare well if the breadwinner of the family dies. If that were true, then these individuals would have less of a need to buy life insurance privately. This hypothesis, however, would imply a kink in the coverage-wealth relationship in Figure2. Coverage should be zero or close to zero for the lowest levels of wealth, and increase steeply after some wealth level at which the social insurance benefits no longer are accessible. As the figure shows, however, there is no such kink in the functional form of life insurance coverage as a function of wealth.
So far we have documented a cross-sectional pattern: People who have more wealth during our 43-month sample have more extensive insurance coverage, a result that contradicts classic models of insurance choice. Later in the paper, we attempt to explain this puzzling result by accounting for channels not captured in existing theories, including wealth-related differences in risk preferences, pricing, background risk, litigation risk, liquidity constraints, financial acumen, or employer-provided benefits in our cross-sectional regressions. However, it is possible that omitted variables that positively correlate with both wealth and a person's insurance choices are not captured by the cross-sectional analyses presented thus far. We investigate this possibility by including individual fixed effects and reestimating our models for having life insurance, as well as for the extent of life insurance coverage. Here we make use of the fact that our raw data are at the person-month level.
Measuring variables such as financial wealth or the consumption needs of dependents based on monthly data comes with more noise than measuring these variables for one person by averaging across all of the months when the individual is in the sample. This can naturally lead to attenuation in coefficients estimated in a person fixed-effects specification using person-month data, as opposed to a cross-sectional analysis at the person level. In addition to coefficient attenuation coming from potential noise in the measurement of financial wealth at a monthly frequency, the estimated coefficients in within-person regressions may be smaller in magnitude due to unobserved fixed costs of rebalancing insurance holdings (or perhaps inertia, as in Sydnor (2010) and Koijen, Van Nieuwerburgh, and Yogo (2016)). Individuals may not reoptimize their portfolios in response to small changes in their circumstances due to effort costs or other portfolio-rebalancing costs. Because we do not observe the term length of the insurance contracts, we are unable to observe when individuals naturally reevaluate their insurance decisions, and thus it would not be surprising if the coverage limit does not respond to month-to-month changes in financial wealth, spending, or other characteristics to the same extent as in the results discussed earlier. Such lack of responsiveness would result in the coefficient estimates being attenuated in the person-month data relative to the cross-sectional analyses. That being said, even if when accounting for individual fixed effects we continue to see a significant and positive correlation between wealth and insurance coverage, this would tell us that the puzzle that we document above is not driven by an omitted variable that is a fixed characteristic of an individual.
The results in TableVshow that, even accounting for person fixed effects, as well as the controls used in the main regressions, we continue to estimate a positive and significant effect of wealth on insurance coverage. Specifically, as indicated by the results in the first column, increasing a person's financial wealth by $100,000 in a given month leads to a 2 percentage point increase in the probability that the person has a life insurance policy in that month. In the cross-sectional analysis in TableII, we find that if a person had $100,000 more financial wealth than another person, on average across the 43 months in the sample, the first individual would have a 12 percentage point higher probability of having life insurance relative to the second individual at some point during the sample period. The estimate in the second column (0.05) shows that within-person, a $1 increase in financial wealth leads to a $0.05 increase in the life insurance coverage limit observed in a given month. The corresponding coefficient in the cross-sectional analysis in TableIVis $0.64, which is an order of magnitude higher than the $0.05 estimated in the person fixed effects specification. In terms of the effects of within-person changes in housing wealth on insurance coverage, we obtain positive and significant estimates in all specifications in TableV, with magnitudes similar to those observed in our cross-sectional analyses.
We next investigate whether the results in TableVare driven by either the intensive or extensive margin of insurance purchases. To do so, we split the sample into individuals who hold life insurance throughout the sample period and those who do not and reestimate the model in column (2) of TableV. The results, reported inAppendixTableA.IVshow that the estimated coefficients on financial wealth in the two samples are similar in magnitude (0.038 for the individuals who have always held life insurance and 0.048 for everyone else), but the former is not statistically significant. Thus, it appears that the within-person results in column (2) of TableVare driven primarily by individuals who choose to purchase new life insurance policies as they become more wealthy or to let policies lapse as they become less wealthy.
How do we interpret the estimated effects of wealth on insurance coverage that we obtain from these within-person regressions? First, all of these estimates are positive, economically meaningful, and statistically significant. This suggests that omitted, fixed individual characteristics cannot explain the puzzling result that wealthier people in our sample are better insured. Second, the fact that the effects of financial wealth on insurance coverage are smaller when using a person fixed effects specification than when conducting cross-sectional analyses indicates that there may be certain differences between people who are wealthier and those who are not that lead wealthier individuals to be better insured.
We turn next to potential mechanisms that can explain the correlation that we document thus far. To do so, we use cross-sectional data. Due to the short nature of our panel, there is insufficient within-person variation in characteristics related to the mechanisms we investigate (e.g., financial literacy) we therefore rely on cross-sectional analyses.
article.IV. Results: Why Are Wealth and Insuranc:
Standard models of insurance or portfolio choice are built on the assumption of decreasing (absolute) risk aversion. While unlikely, it might be the case that in our data wealthier individuals are more risk-averse, which would lead to empirical patterns of insurance choice that run in the opposite direction of what the standard models predict. We can test this explanation by examining the extent to which the individuals in our sample allocate their wealth to risky financial assets. We calculate the dollar amount of funds allocated by each individual to risk-free accounts, including savings and checking accounts, and certificates of deposit. We also calculate the dollar amount of funds that the same person allocates to risky assets, through investment accounts offered by the financial institution providing the data.16These accounts include regular brokerage accounts as well as tax-advantaged investment accounts, and contain investments in mutual funds, stocks, and bonds. Figure3plots the dollar amount (left panel) and the share (right panel) allocated to risky assets as a function of the total amount invested with the financial institution across safe and risky financial assets. The figure clearly shows that risk tolerance increases with wealth, as typically assumed by the theory and as documented by prior studies on portfolio allocations that use survey or administrative data (see Guiso and Sodini (2013) for an overview of that literature).
To further investigate whether accounting for wealth-dependent risk preferences might explain the puzzling positive correlation between wealth and insurance coverage, in TableA.Vof theAppendix, we reestimate our baseline models for life and homeowners coverage using the share of account balances held in risky assets as an additional explanatory variable using the subsample of individuals that employ the wealth management arm of the financial institution providing the data. Controlling for this measure of risk tolerance does not qualitatively affect our results—we continue to find that wealthier people have more life insurance, controlling for the value of the asset insured, that is, the consumption stream of dependents.
Another preference-related channel that may be at play when people buy insurance is ambiguity aversion. Prior work shows theoretically that higher ambiguity aversion generally increases the demand for insurance (Alary, Gollier, and Treich (2013)), which is intuitive, as increases in ambiguity aversion have effects similar to increased pessimism. For ambiguity aversion to explain the positive wealth-insurance correlation that we document in the data, it must be the case that ambiguity aversion increases with wealth. Baillon and Placido (2019) conduct an experiment investigating the wealth-ambiguity preferences relationship. They find no clear patterns in terms of increasing, decreasing, or constant ambiguity aversion attitudes toward wealth. While we do not have proxies in our data that measure ambiguity-related preferences, we argue based on this prior work that our results are not likely driven by the wealthy being more ambiguity-averse than the less wealthy.
We also consider whether prospect theory might help explain the positive wealth-insurance correlation. Under prospect theory, wealth does not directly affect demand for insurance because the prospect theory function evaluates gains and losses (i.e., changes in wealth) relative to some reference point. Thus, a household with $1,000,000 in wealth evaluates a potential loss of $1,000 in the same way as a household with $100,000 in wealth. Gottlieb and Mitchell (2020) examine demand for long-term care insurance using the HRS and find that agents who they classify as acting according to prospect theory have lower demand for long-term care insurance.17The authors find no correlation between wealth and their measure of whether an agent acts according to prospect theory. Based on this prior work, we infer that it is unlikely that the positive wealth-insurance correlation we document is driven by the existence of a correlation between wealth and having prospect theory-like preferences.
In canonical models of life insurance demand, such as that briefly described in SectionI, the price paid per dollar of life insurance coverage does not depend on the wealth of the insured at the time the policy is bought. In practice, however, it is possible that the pricing is wealth dependent.
Consider the framework proposed in Rampini and Viswanathan (2019). In that model, wealthier households face lower insurance prices to hedge future income risk. This is driven by the assumption that an increase in households' income decreases the probability of low-income realizations in the future. It is important to keep in mind that savings and insurance are the same concept in that setting, as households' current wealth is current-period income (from both an exogenous income process and payoffs from prior-period insurance purchases). Households use income in a given period to consume or to buy insurance through Arrow-Debreu claims. Hence, buying insurance is the only means to save for the future. The authors treat different insurance products identically by considering all insurable losses as income shocks to the household (i.e., the authors consider the loss of a labor income earner—covered by life insurance—or a insurable shock to a home's value to be an income shock from the perspective of a household).18While the model is stark, it does feature a channel that seems reasonable: insurance for future states that are less likely should be cheaper. Since higher income individuals are likely to have a lower probability of having a bad income shock in the future, getting insurance against those bad states should be cheaper for them than for people who are currently in a lower income state.
To date there is no evidence in the literature on whether insurers take individuals' income into account when pricing life insurance policies or other insurance products. Our data allow us to study whether prices for coverage in fact vary across people based on their income or wealth. We investigate this pricing channel in TableVI. The dependent variable in the ordinary least squares regressions in this table is the price (premium) paid annually by an individual per dollar of life insurance coverage.
In this analysis, we include two control variables expected to impact the price paid per dollar of coverage: the coverage limit (to allow for economies of scale) and the probability of death in the next year (to account for the fact that individuals facing a higher risk of death have to pay a higher price for life insurance, all else equal). These control variables do indeed have significant effects on the price paid per dollar of life insurance coverage, and the directions of these effects are as we would expect: People with a higher probability of death, and people buying a smaller amount of coverage in their term life insurance policies pay a higher price per dollar of coverage secured. The latter result stems from the fact that part of the premiums paid by insureds are to cover monitoring and other fixed costs paid by the insurer, implying the potential for economies of scale with respect to coverage on individuals. These results also serve to validate our measure of insurance prices.
Having cleared these basic checks, we examine the variables of interest, namely, the financial and housing wealth of the individuals in the sample, and how these correlate with the prices paid for life insurance coverage. We find that, controlling for the amount and probability of loss insured, individuals with higher financial wealth pay higher prices for life insurance. A similar effect appears if we replace financial wealth with the person's average annual income during the sample period: Higher income individuals pay higher premiums for a certain level of life insurance coverage. At first sight, this result seems contrary to the mechanism in Rampini and Viswanathan (2019). However, a possible explanation that can reconcile this empirical result with their model is that wealthier individuals may choose term life insurance policies that have a longer term, that is, offer protection for a given level of coverage limit over more years than the policies secured by less wealthy individuals. This would lead to wealthier individuals paying higher premiums per dollar of coverage. Under this scenario, the wealthier get more coverage, in the sense of the time frame covered, aside from the dollar limit of coverage.
It is also possible that wealthier people buy more insurance because they have a stronger bequest motive (e.g., a stronger interest in passing on a legacy), given the evidence in Koijen, Van Nieuwerburgh, and Yogo (2016) of a positive correlation between the strength of the bequest motive and financial wealth. This could explain why wealth and life insurance coverage are positively correlated, if the positive impact of wealth on the bequest motive effect were stronger than its negative impact on risk aversion.
To address this concern, we conduct two additional analyses. First, we examine whether or not wealthier individuals in our sample are more likely to have children, which may itself be a measure of bequest motive intensity (e.g., desire to leave a legacy). We split individuals into terciles of total wealth (financial wealth plus housing wealth) and find that, controlling for age, gender, marital status, and state fixed effects, the wealthiest households have a similar probability of having children as the middle third of households, of approximately 75%.
Second, we more directly test for differences in bequest motive intensity among wealthier individuals by reestimating coefficients from the model in TableIV, column (4) (life insurance coverage limits), separately for low-, medium-, and high-wealth individuals. The intensity of the bequest motive is measured by the coefficient on the insured asset (i.e., the coefficient on the future consumption of dependents, which is $0.04 in the full sample). As Figure4shows, we find that bequest motive intensity does not differ significantly across the low-, medium-, and high-wealth individuals in our data. While we cannot definitively rule out a positive correlation between wealth and bequest motive intensity, this evidence suggests that bequest motives are not so strong among the wealthiest families to be the driving force behind our results.
Another possible reason why wealthier people get higher insurance coverage is that they face more background risk. Perhaps the shocks to future consumption, income, or wealth that the wealthier households face come from a wider distribution compared to the shocks that less wealthy households face. As shown theoretically by Fischer (1973) and Rampini and Viswanathan (2019), increasing income volatility can increase the demand for insurance, whether it is life- or property-related. Similarly, consumption volatility may be higher for wealthier individuals, and hence insurance may be more important for these individuals. More generally, an independent and uninsurable background risk causes individuals to increase their demand for insurance (see, for example, Gollier and Pratt (1996)).19The effects of nonindependent background risk on households' demand for insurance naturally depends on the combination of preferences and the relationship between insurable and background risks. See Schlesinger (2013) for further discussion on the effects of both independent and nonindependent background risk on insurance demand.
We examine this volatility-related mechanism in TableVII. We find that in general, consumption, income, and wealth are more volatile in absolute dollar terms for individuals with higher financial or housing wealth. We calculate annualized volatilities for each individual by taking the standard deviation of the monthly values (over time) and multiplying by the square root of 12.20The estimates in TableVIIshow that a $1 increase in financial wealth is associated with a $0.03 increase in the annualized volatility of consumption. The wealthy also have more volatile income processes: A $1.00 increase in financial wealth is associated with a $0.18 increase in the annualized volatility of income. It is therefore possible that one factor driving the positive link that we observe between wealth and insurance coverage is the increased background risk of more affluent households.
To investigate this possibility further, we add these measures of background risk as additional explanatory variables in our models in which the dependent variable is the extent of life insurance coverage. The analysis is presented in TableVIII. As predicted by theory, consumption volatility and income volatility are significant, positive predictors of insurance coverage limits, that is, people that face more background risk have more insurance coverage. As shown in Figure5, these results hold throughout the entire distribution of consumption or income volatility. However, while controlling for background risk has a slight impact on the effects of financial or housing wealth on insurance coverage limits relative to those estimated in our baseline model in TableIV, the effects remain positive, and both statistically and economically significant. The evidence therefore indicates that the higher background risk faced by wealthier individuals cannot itself explain why wealthier individuals have more insurance coverage.
Differences in subjective beliefs regarding the probability of insurable losses between poorer and wealthier individuals may result in different coverage decisions. For example, wealthy individuals may purchase more life insurance coverage because they believe that they are likely to die sooner. Puri and Robinson (2007) develop a measure of individual subjective optimism, defined as the difference between individuals' expected longevity versus longevity as predicted by actuarial life tables. The authors show that this measure of subjective optimism is positively correlated with wealth. Accordingly, wealthy individuals should be less likely to purchase life insurance based on their subjective beliefs. It is therefore improbable that wealthier people in our sample have higher insurance coverage because of more pessimistic subjective beliefs about future shocks, relative to less wealthy individuals.
Another potential mechanism for our results relates to adverse selection—wealthier people could buy more life insurance because they know they face more risk in terms of life expectancy. However, prior work suggests that this mechanism is unlikely. As shown by Cawley and Philipson (1999) in the context of life insurance or Finkelstein and McGarry (2006) for long-term care insurance, there is no evidence of adverse selection in these products. Rather, these papers find that people who face less risk are the ones who buy more insurance.
It is also possible that wealthier individuals have more insurance coverage because they are more aware of the existence of insurance products. The effects of financial acumen on household economic choices have been studied by prior literature (see the review in Gomes, Haliassos, and Ramadorai (2021)), with newer research showing a causal effect of exposure to certain financial products on choices. For example, people with more experience with banking services early in life have better consumer credit outcomes years later (Brown, Cookson, and Heimer (2019)), while people with more access to information regarding financial markets subsequently have better exposure to equities (Haliassos, Jansson, and Karabulut (2020)).
Here, we use education as our proxy for financial knowledge, specifically, for knowledge regarding insurance products. In our data, we know whether or not a person has a college degree for about one-third of the sample. Education is not a personal characteristic that generally has to be shared by a consumer when they open a checking account or a brokerage account, for example. However, some of the clients of the institution providing the data revealed their education level. We split the sample into individuals without a college degree, those for whom the education level is unknown, and those who have a college degree or postcollege education.
We investigate whether the positive correlation between wealth and insurance coverage weakens or disappears after controlling for education. As the regression results in Panel A of TableIXshow, however, this is not the case. We observe that, all else equal, people who have a college degree have $44,000 higher coverage limits for term life insurance. One interpretation of this result is that education helps people be aware of, or interested in, using these types of products. Another interpretation is that higher education is a proxy for higher expected future earnings. In the context of life insurance, this may correlate with dependents having higher expected consumption that needs to be insured through this product. Thus, while education could proxy for financial literacy, it may also correlate with other factors that would make a person choose a higher level of coverage. That being said, irrespective of what characteristics education captures, the results in TableIXshow that controlling for whether a person has a college degree does not eliminate the strong, positive correlation between financial or housing wealth and the extent of life insurance coverage.
Related to the financial literacy driver of insurance purchase decisions, it is possible that the less wealthy do not trust financial products or institutions as much as their more wealthy counterparts, and as a result do not purchase products like insurance. It is also possible that insurance products are advertised more to those who are wealthier, in which case, there could be supply-side reasons for why these individuals have better insurance coverage. While these are important potential mechanisms at play in households' insurance purchase decisions, we cannot test them with the data available to us at this point.
Another potential reason wealthier people buy more insurance is that they may not have generous benefits through their job. For example, wealthier individuals may be more likely to be self-employed, and thus may not have access to employer-provided life insurance. While we intuitively think of wealthier people as having better benefits through their job compared to their less wealthy peers, it is worth investigating if in our data this intuition may not be correct.
The individuals in our sample work for over 40,000 unique employers. The employer most represented in the sample is the U.S. federal government. About one-third of individuals in our sample receive labor income from the federal or a local government at some point during the four-year sample period. We can therefore conduct a within-employer analysis of the relationship between wealth and insurance coverage. In this analysis, we keep constant the benefits (such as group life insurance) that employees get through their job and examine whether wealth continues to positively predict the extent of insurance purchased by these individuals privately.
In column (1) of TableIX, Panel B, we observe that the significant, positive correlation between wealth and insurance coverage continues to persist, even when we examine the subsample of individuals who are U.S. federal or local government employees. The second column conducts the same analysis, but for the individuals in the sample who are not employed by the U.S. federal or a local government. Interestingly, controlling for all of the factors considered in the main analysis in TableIV, the correlation between financial wealth and life insurance coverage limit is greater in magnitude in the sample of people who do not have jobs with the government. A possible reason is that certain factors that may drive insurance demand, like background risk, could be different for people working in nongovernment versus government jobs.
It may be the case that individuals, particularly those with low income and low financial wealth, are liquidity-constrained and unable to afford insurance due to other financial commitments. Prior literature generally finds that liquidity constraints reduce household spending (e.g., Zeldes (1989), Johnson, Parker, and Souleles (2006), Baker and Yannelis (2017)), particularly for households with low financial sophistication (Parker (2017)).21Buying insurance, however, is not simply spending—it is a risk-mitigation strategy used for consumption smoothing. As a result, as Gollier (2003) and Ericson and Sydnor (2018) show theoretically, insurance products should be most sought after by individuals who are most liquidity-constrained (particularly those products involving the smooth payment of small premiums over time rather than the payment of one large lump-sum premium).
However, the model of Rampini and Viswanathan (2019) predict that the marginal utility of consumption may be sufficiently high for low-wealth individuals (who are more likely to be liquidity-constrained) such that they optimally choose to purchase less insurance. In their model, wealth and insurance demand are positively correlated across the entire cross-section of households. Since insurance premia must be paid in advance, the authors point out that insurance can be viewed as state-contingent savings. In that setting, households optimize over current consumption and one-period-ahead savings by purchasing Arrow-Debreu securities that pay off in different income states (i.e., insurance contracts). Poorer households value current consumption more than wealthier households and therefore save less (i.e., purchase fewer Arrow-Debreu securities). Ericson and Sydnor (2018) and Casaburi and Willis (2018) also point out that the timing and frequency of premium payments affect the value of insurance, particularly for liquidity-constrained individuals. These theoretical insights together emphasize the intertemporal aspect of insurance choice. Relative to poorer households, wealthier households may be better able to bear risk, but they are also better able to smooth consumption over time (which raises the motive to save today, potentially via insurance). These two channels operate in opposite directions: The risk-bearing channel implies increased demand for insurance among the poor, but the consumption-smoothing channel predicts decreased demand for insurance among the poor.
While we are unable to fully test these authors' models, our evidence is consistent with them. We find that wealthier individuals are less liquidity-constrained and that lower liquidity corresponds to a lower level of insurance coverage. We measure liquidity constraints for each individual by constructing what we refer to as the credit use ratio. We define the credit use ratio as monthly expenditures on the credit card provided by the financial institution divided by the credit limit on the card.22Figure6, and Panel A of TableX, show that in general that the credit use ratio decreases with financial wealth. We also observe a generally decreasing relationship between wealth and credit usage for most of the housing wealth distribution.
As predicted, in Panel B of TableX, we find that those who are most liquidity-constrained have lower insurance coverage and are more likely to let their policies lapse. The credit use ratio positively predicts policy lapsation and negatively predicts insurance coverage limits, as expected to the extent that liquidity constraints impact people's ability to pay their insurance premiums.
However, the regression models in Panel B of TableXalso indicate that even after controlling for a person's credit use ratio, we continue to observe a strong and positive correlation between wealth (financial or housing-related) and the extent of their life insurance coverage. The estimates in the first column indicate that after controlling for liquidity constraints, a $100,000 increase in financial wealth is associated with a 3 percentage points decrease in the probability of life insurance lapsing, and a $100,000 increase in housing wealth is associated with a 0.5 percentage points decrease in the probability of lapsing life insurance. The estimates in the last column in the table show that a $1.00 increase in financial wealth is associated with a $0.63 increase in life insurance coverage, an effect similar to that in TableIV($0.64). In other words, liquidity or the lack thereof cannot explain away the puzzling fact that wealthier people are better insured.
While this analysis focuses on households' current liquidity constraints, it is possible that concerns about future access to liquidity may also be salient for life insurance decisions. Lenders' willingness to extend credit to households is a function of their expected income, which is likely to decrease if a wage-earner dies. This could make it difficult for dependents to borrow money from lenders to smooth consumption. Accordingly, life insurance purchases, by replacing the breadwinner's income stream in the event of their death, alleviate the potential loss of access to financing. This may be the mechanism behind the patterns seen in TablesII,III, andIV. These analyses show that individuals with mortgages and dependents are more likely to own life insurance, are less likely to lapse, and have greater coverage all else equal. For these individuals, life insurance may serve as a hedge against the possibility of death and the binding liquidity constraints that would result for dependents, who may not be able to raise sufficient financing to service the existing mortgage on their home.
Overall, the results in this section show that certain frictions not directly addressed by models of insurance help explain some of the positive correlation that we document between wealth and life insurance coverage but cannot fully explain this main effect. So far, we have examined the role of various frictions separately. InAppendixTableA.VI, we test whether controlling for the factors discussed here simultaneously can explain the puzzling positive wealth-insurance relationship. We find that the estimated relationship between financial wealth and insurance coverage is positive, economically meaningful, and statistically significant even after simultaneously controlling for all of the mechanisms discussed above.
article.V. Implications and Conclusion:
While a significant theoretical literature focuses on households' insurance purchase decisions, empirical evidence, especially based on administrative data, is scarce. Insurance products are present in the financial portfolio of the majority of households, yet it remains unclear how people make coverage decisions in practice. In this paper, we use a rich administrative data set to test one of the most salient theoretical predictions about the drivers of insurance choices, focusing on the market for term life insurance.
Specifically, we test the theoretical prediction that in a setting in which risk aversion decreases with wealth, individuals who are wealthier are more likely to self-insure relative to those less well-off. The preference for self-insurance is driven by the fact that insurance products come with a load, that is, monitoring and other costs that lead the prices paid for these products to be actuarially unfair. We document a stark pattern in our sample of 63,000 individuals that contradicts the theory: Life insurance coverage increases with wealth, controlling for the value of the insured asset. Moreover, wealthier individuals are less likely to let their life insurance policies lapse. In specifications using approximately 2,500,000 person-month observations, in which we include person-level fixed effects, we again estimate a significant, positive correlation between wealth and insurance coverage, indicating that as people become wealthier, they also become better insured.
We investigate several mechanisms that could help explain this puzzling finding, including wealth-related differences in risk preferences, pricing, background risk, bequest motives, financial literacy, employer-provided benefits, and liquidity constraints. We find that these mechanisms do explain some of the positive correlation between wealth and insurance coverage. In particular, poorer individuals may forgo insurance due to liquidity constraints and wealthier individuals may purchase more insurance due to higher background risk. Nevertheless, we continue to observe that the wealthy are better insured even after accounting for these factors.
Overall, these results suggest that more work is needed to understand households' insurance choices. While we examine several potential mechanisms, our results suggest that there are other wealth-related reasons why those who are better off financially also have better insurance coverage. Identifying these explanations represents a fruitful avenue for future work using household-level data. From a more macroeconomics-oriented perspective, it is possible that the wealth-related differences in insurance coverage that we observe may vary with the business cycle, or may lead to heterogeneity in the effects of macroeconomic shocks across different segments of the population. Our data only cover four years, which do not include a recession, and thus we cannot investigate these possibilities. From a policy perspective, it also remains to be understood whether wealthier households are overinsured, or the less wealthy are underinsured. For example, it is important to determine whether the provision of insurance products by financial institutions varies by wealth, with poorer households not having the same access to insurance products as their wealthier counterparts. If there is a preferential supply of insurance products to the wealthy, who theoretically need these products the least, this could imply that poorer households are underserved by insurance companies and will not be protected when negative shocks arrive in the future. We hope that future work can address these questions related to insurance coverage, which is a critical aspect of households' financial health.
Editors: Antoinette Schoar, Urban Jermann, Leonid Kogan, Jonathan Lewellen, and Thomas Philippon

AItitle: 财富悖论挑战经典模型：流动性约束驱动跨期保险需求
AIsummary: **论文总结**  该研究聚焦家庭金融领域，探讨财富如何影响人寿保险选择这一传统理论悖论——经典模型认为高财富人群因风险承受力强会减少投保，但实证数据却显示相反趋势。基于63万个体、250万次观测的行政数据，作者发现即便控制被保险人资产价值（如家属消费需求）、风险偏好、流动性约束等变量，财富与保险覆盖仍呈显著正相关。与传统模型（Mossin等）强调保险仅用于跨状态风险分摊不同，作者引入时间维度，验证流动性约束理论（Rampini等）：穷人因现金流限制难以预付保费，而富人可通过保险实现跨期消费平滑。研究通过固定效应模型排除个体异质性干扰，并检验定价、遗产动机等潜在机制，但最终仍有部分相关性无法解释，暗示供给端因素或未建模的行为动机需进一步探索。
Highlights:
学术要点 1：时间状态平滑（Consumption-Smoothing Across Time） 突破传统保险仅关注跨状态风险分散的框架，提出保险通过预付保费机制实现跨时间资源转移，解释财富与保险的正相关。|||
学术要点 2：背景风险异质性（Background Risk Heterogeneity） 财富差异导致家庭面临不同分布的经济冲击（如消费/收入波动），高财富者通过增加保险覆盖应对更复杂的风险组合。|||
学术要点 3：状态依赖储蓄（State-Contingent Savings） 将保险重构为基于状态的时间维度储蓄工具，强调流动性约束对保费支付能力的限制作用，区别于传统风险规避解释。|||
学术要点 4：保险定价的期限结构效应（Term Structure Effect in Pricing） 高财富者可能选择更长期限保单，导致单位保额保费更高，揭示财富与保险价格的非对称关系。|||
学术要点 5：保单失效动态（Policy Lapsation Dynamics） 首次在行政数据中捕捉保险持续性特征，发现财富通过流动性约束渠道降低失效概率，揭示保险覆盖质量的财富梯度。
